coredata.dc:title: Coordination engineering of defective F and N co-doped carbon dots anchored silver nanoparticles for boosting oxygen reduction reaction
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chu, Siyu
Sun, Min
Li, Zijiong
Wang, Haiyan
Miao, Feng
coredata.dc:description: The carbon-based catalysts exhibit excellent electrocatalytic and stable performance toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), which are expected to replace expensive and scarce Pt-based catalysts. However, how to regulate the interaction between silver and carrier carbon dots (CD) to improve electrocatalytic performance toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and stability, has not been systematically studied. In this study, we in-situ synthesized fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CD anchoring Ag nanoparticles by UV irradiation reduction method. The peak potential, onset-potential and half-wave potential of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 are 0.85 V, 1.056 V, and 0.9 V, respectively, indicating outstanding ORR performance. The current density of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 remains 97.5 % after 50, 000 s, showing more excellent stability than Pt/C. The impressive ORR catalytic activity and stability of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 could be attributed to the strong anchoring effect of nitrogen-doped CD on Ag. This effect leads to a significant interaction and charge transfer between Ag and CD, thereby enhancing the charge transfer process during the catalytic reaction. This project aims to design a carbon dot/silver composite catalyst with high ORR catalytic performance and favorable stability. The implementation of this project provides good theoretical guidance for the design of carbon nanomaterial-based catalytic systems.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Oxygen reduction reaction
Catalyst
Co-doped

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of F, N-CD@Ag electrocatalysts.

TEM images of the (a) F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, (d) F, N-CD@Ag-0.1, (g) F, N-CD@Ag-0.2; HRTEM images of the (b) F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, (e) F, N-CD@Ag-0.1, (h) F, N-CD@Ag-0.2; Size distribution of (c) F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, (f) F, N-CD@Ag-0.1, (i) F, N-CD@Ag-0.2.
TEM elemental mapping of C (red), N (purple), O(green), Ag(yellow)of (b) F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, (c) F, N-CD@Ag-0.1and (d) F, N-CD@Ag-0.2, respectively.
(a) XRD and (b)Raman spectra patterns of F, N-CD, F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2, respectively.
(a)Ag 3d spectra and (b)N 1 s spectra and (c)F 1 s spectra of F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2, respectively; (d) The content of.

(a) CV, (b) LSV and (c) The onset potential, half-wave potential and limited current density of F, N-CD@Ag; (d) RDE voltammetry curves of the F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 for ORR at rotation speeds ranging from 400 to 2025 rpm; (e) K-L plots of J−1 versus ω−1/2 at different voltages; (f) The number of electron transfers during oxygen reduction reaction under different voltages; (g) Tafel slopes; (h) Nyquist plots measured at 0.86 V vs RHE; (i) The Gibbs free energy step diagram of ORR reaction pathway of F, N-CD and F, N-CD@Ag.
The CV curves before and after 5000 cycles of F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2; (d) The Amperometric i-t curves of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 and Pt/C in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution.

head.ce:title: Coordination engineering of defective F and N co-doped carbon dots anchored silver nanoparticles for boosting oxygen reduction reaction

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Electronics and Information, Zhengzhou University of Light Industry

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The carbon-based catalysts exhibit excellent electrocatalytic and stable performance toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR), which are expected to replace expensive and scarce Pt-based catalysts. However, how to regulate the interaction between silver and carrier carbon dots (CD) to improve electrocatalytic performance toward oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and stability, has not been systematically studied. In this study, we in-situ synthesized fluorine and nitrogen co-doped CD anchoring Ag nanoparticles by UV irradiation reduction method. The peak potential, onset-potential and half-wave potential of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 are 0.85 V, 1.056 V, and 0.9 V, respectively, indicating outstanding ORR performance. The current density of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 remains 97.5 % after 50, 000 s, showing more excellent stability than Pt/C. The impressive ORR catalytic activity and stability of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 could be attributed to the strong anchoring effect of nitrogen-doped CD on Ag. This effect leads to a significant interaction and charge transfer between Ag and CD, thereby enhancing the charge transfer process during the catalytic reaction. This project aims to design a carbon dot/silver composite catalyst with high ORR catalytic performance and favorable stability. The implementation of this project provides good theoretical guidance for the design of carbon nanomaterial-based catalytic systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy and the environment have become urgent problems for mankind in the 21st century, and the emergence and development of Fuel Cell is of great strategic importance to alleviate environmental pollution and energy crisis [1]. Oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is an important reaction in new energy storage and conversion systems such as alkaline fuel cells and aluminum-air batteries [2]. However, the cumbersome and slow electron transfer step of ORR results in low mass transfer efficiency and slow kinetics, which greatly hinders its application in practical devices. Therefore, it is necessary to develop efficient ORR catalysts that can simultaneously achieve fast charge transfer and mass transfer. The commonly used cathode catalysts are precious metal materials like platinum [3–5], which greatly increases their cost and limits their large-scale application. Consequently, developing efficient, stable, and cost-effective carbon-based catalysts for oxygen reduction has become one of the breakthrough points in accelerating the commercial application process of fuel cells [6–8].

Carbon-based nanomaterials with transition metal catalysts have low cost, high catalytic activity, and good stability, and have great potential in replacing precious metal oxygen reduction catalysts in fuel cells [9–11]. Carbon dots (CD) possess remarkable electron donor/acceptor properties, inherent structural defects, and plentiful electrocatalytic active sites among countless carbon nanomaterials. These unique characteristics offer promising potential for their utilization in electrocatalytic applications, including water electrolysis, oxygen evolution reaction (OER), ORR, and carbon dioxide reduction reaction (CO2-RR) [12,13]. On the one hand, CD can be easily obtained by a green and low-energy consumption preparation process, and its electrocatalytic activity can be further enhanced by doping heteroatoms to modulate the electronic structure and charge distribution of CD; On the other hand, due to the presence of empty d-orbitals and various functional groups, CD can not only anchor the size adjustable transition metal silver on the surface, but also transfer charges between the CD and the anchored metal silver. The synergistic effect between the two can significantly reduce the energy barrier of the electrocatalytic reaction process, thereby improving the electrocatalytic performance and stability of the catalytic system, and reducing costs.

In recent years, significant progress has mainly focused on exploring new synthesis methods, precisely modifying and adjusting the micro/meso/macro structure and surface/edge state of CD, developing efficient multi-component composite materials, controlling the aggregation/assembly behavior of CD, and analyzing the structure performance relationship and mechanism of CD-based materials as advanced electrode materials. Tam Tran Van et. al[14] prepared graphene hydrogel/B-doped carbon dot composite (GH-BGQD) by one-step hydrothermal method. The hybrid carbon material showed outstanding ORR performance. The outstanding activity of GH-BGQD is attributed to the abundant active sites of B-doped carbon dots, which provide high oxygen adsorption energy and promote the binding during O2 oxygen reduction. In summary, CD has a large specific surface area, and rich surface/edge functional groups, which can serve as active centers and provide bonding space [15,16], providing more nucleation sites for the growth of metal nanoparticles, and enabling effective and controllable doping of heteroatoms, thereby improving the charge transfer rate and stability of composite materials. By effectively controlling the electronic structure, the electrocatalytic reaction rate can be improved [17,18]. Above all, CD has unique edge structures, good conductivity, and synergistic effects with heteroatoms, which makes it a great potential for application in electrocatalysis.

Anchoring metal silver with CD can not only improve the stability and conductivity of catalysts but also enhance the dispersion of active sites of metal atoms. Monireh Faraji's research team [19] prepared a graphene quantum dot-supported manganese oxide and silver oxygen reduction electrocatalytic system using an electrochemical deposition method. The initial reduction potential was about 1.0 V, the half-wave potential was 0.867 V, and the diffusion current density was 5.65 mA cm−2, which was close to the electrocatalytic performance of commercial Pt/C. After 10, 000 seconds, the activity only decreased by 5.6 %, far superior to Pt/C (84.7 %). As expected, Chatenet et al.[20] have previously proposed in their paper that Ag/C transfers 4 electrons per O2 molecule during the ORR process. Kim et al.[21] prepared Ag with two types of structures, nano dendrites and nanowires, through immersion plasma discharge. The latter showed a more favorable trend for ORR, which could be due to its defect structure providing more adsorption centers for oxygen.

To adjust the electronic configuration and spin state of the central atom (transition metal), and enhance the catalytic activity and stability of the system, heteroatoms with lone pair electrons (such as nitrogen atoms, sulfur atoms, etc.) are usually introduced into the carbon matrix [22,23], or surface modification (defect control) is performed on the carbon matrix [24]. The d-band filled with metallic silver is generally considered to catalyze slowly in most reactions and exhibits certain limitations in catalysis, with relatively low specific activity and a negative half-wave potential shift of about 200 mV compared to Pt/C catalysts. But people can effectively adjust the center position of the silver d-band through the Metal Support Interaction (MSI) effect to strengthen the Ag-O interaction, making it easier to break the O-O bond and enhance its ORR kinetics [25,26]. Moreover, silver is relatively low-cost and abundant in reserves, and is considered one of the alternatives to platinum-based precious metal catalysts. Research shows that by regulating the morphology and crystal structure of silver nanoparticles, electrocatalytic reactions can occur on specific crystal planes, thereby optimizing the oxygen reduction catalytic performance of the system [27–29].

In summary, the regulation of carriers, the interaction between carriers and metals, and the morphology, size, and crystal structure of nanoparticles all have a significant impact on the electrocatalytic performance. Although some progress has been made in the research of composite metal electrochemical catalysts using CD as a carrier, so far, there has been no systematic research on how to regulate the interaction between metal silver and carrier CD to improve the oxygen reduction electrocatalytic performance and stability. This project aims to use CD as a carrier to anchor controllable silver nanoparticles, achieving controllable adjustment of the electronic configuration and spin state of transition metal silver; doping or defect control of CD matrix is carried out to achieve controllable adjustment of transition metal silver electronic configuration and spin state through MSI effect, thereby enhancing the oxygen reduction electrocatalytic performance and stability of the system.

Citric acid (CA) monohydrate, urea, ammonium fluoride (NH4F), Nafion (5 wt%), and AgNO3 were purchased from Aladdin. Commercial Pt/C (20 %) is manufactured by Johnson Matthey Company. DI water (18.2 MΩ*cm) was produced in our laboratory.

Fluorine and nitrogen (F, N) co-doped carbon dot (CD) catalysts synthesized by our previous report described elsewhere [30]. F, N-CD catalysts were prepared by taking the optimized mass ratios 1:3 of CD to respective precursors based on the earlier report.

F, N-CD@Ag catalysts were produced by using F, N-CD as a carrier, and AgNO3 as a source of Ag. Reduction of the subsequent admixtures was carried out in a UV irradiation environment at 365 nm for 2 h. The as-synthesized samples were named F, N-CD@Ag. Additionally, different samples were fabricated cause changing the concentration of AgNO3 aqueous solution, were prepared in impregnated into 0.05 M, 0.1 M, and, 0.2 M AgNO3 aqueous solution were designated F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2, respectively.

The microstructures were observed by Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 F). The distributions of elements distribution of catalysts, Ag nanoclusters decoration on the F, N-CD support was picturized by SEM mapping characterization. Raman spectroscopy was conducted to characterize the degree of defects in the sample with an excitation wavelength of 532.08 nm. The crystallinity and particle size of the obtained materials were carried out with an X-ray diffractometer (BRUKER D8Advance) equipped with a Cu-Ka X-ray source. The chemical states and the existence of elements in the electrocatalysts and Ag nanocluster's interaction with the carrier were characterized by an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) apparatus (Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) equipped with a monochromatic Al Ka X-ray source of 1486.6 eV. The specific surface area and the pore size distribution were measured using the nitrogen gas adsorption and desorption method.

All electrochemical measurements were performed using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation. A complete three-electrode system is composed of Pt wire (1 * 37 mm), a saturated calomel electrode (SCE), and a glassy carbon (GC) working electrode (surface area, 0.07065 cm2). The working electrode was prepared as follows: Firstly, the catalyst suspension solution was configurated by dividing 5 mg catalysts in a solution containing 480 μl deionized water, 480 μl ethanol, and 40 μl Nafion solution. 5 μl catalyst solvent was dropped onto a polished GC electrode after the solution was sonicated for 30 min. Cyclic voltammogram (CV) was measured in the potential ranging from 0.1 to 1.2 V in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH with a scan speed of 50 mV s−1. The EIS test was carried out in the frequency range of 100 K Hz ∼ 10 M Hz. The accelerated stability test (ADT) was conducted with a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH to evaluate the durability of catalysts. The ORR activities of catalysts were explored by linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) test at a rotation speed of 1600 rpm at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. Koutecky–Levich plots (j−1 vs. ω−1/2) were analyzed at different electrode potentials. The slope of their optimal linear fit lines was used to calculate the electron transfer number (n) and dynamic current density based on the Koutecky-Levich equation:
1
j
=
1
j
k
+
1
j
d
=
1
j
k
+
1
B
ω
1 / 2
B = 0.2 nF
(
D
o
2
)
2 / 3
v
− 1 / 6
C
o
2
where j represents the measured current density and jk and jd are the kinetic and diffusion limited current densities, respectively. ω is the angular velocity (rpm), n is the transferred electron number, F is the Faraday constant, DO2 is the diffusion coefficient of O2 in 0.1 M KOH (1.86*10−5 cm2 s−1), n is the kinematic viscosity (1.008 *10−2 cm2 s−1), and CO2 is the volume concentration of O2 (1.21*10−6 mol cm−3).

All potentials were reported versus the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE). The potentials versus the SCE electrode were calibrated concerning the SHE using the following equation:
E
SHE
= E
S CE
+ 0.244 + 0.0591 * pH

All calculations were carried out based on density functional theory (DFT) as implemented in the Vienna Ab initio Simulation Package (VASP) with exchange-correlation functional of generalized gradient approximation (GGA) of Perdew, Burke, and Ernzerhof (PBE) method. A grid of 1 × 1 × 1 Monkhorst−Pack k-points was used for the structural relaxation. The computational modes of the F, N-CD, and the F, N-CD@Ag were shown in Fig. S1 and Fig. S2, respectively.

The size of the structural cell model is 29.5 * 25.5 * 15 Å, α=β=γ=90 °, the size of this crystal cell is taken from the size of the expanded graphene cell. By conducting elemental analysis to obtain the proportion of elements and randomly doping them, a C-GQD cluster is formed, F, N-CD@Ag mode is adsorbing an Ag cluster on the surface of C-GQD clusters according to elemental ratios; The distance between two clusters should be greater than 8.5 Å to avoid mirror effects between the two clusters.

A vacuum layer of 15 Å was adopted in the direction perpendicular to the monolayer surface to avoid the interactions between periodic slabs. The energy cutoff was set to be 450 eV. The convergence criterion for the energy and maximum force for the optimization were set to 10−5 eV and 0.05 eV/Å, respectively. The adsorption ability with the surface was evaluated by comparing the adsorption energy, the adsorption energy is defined as:
Eads = Eadsorb/surf − Esurf – Eadsorb

Where Eadsorb/surf, Esurf, and Eadsorb were the calculated total energies of the substrate with adsorbate(s), the clean substrate, and the isolated adsorbate, respectively.The free energy changes were calculated according to the following equation:
△G = △E + △EZPE −T△S
where ΔE, ΔE
ZPE , and ΔS were the differences in total energy, zero-point energy, and entropy between the product and reactants, respectively. Here, we just considered the ground states of species at a temperature of 0 K. So, the contribution from the entropy term was zero.

The hydrothermal method is a common method for preparing CD, which means that the material is synthesized by heating and stirring in an aqueous system. The described method demonstrates a rapid and efficient reaction, proving its significance in catalyst synthesis. Meanwhile, silver ions are adsorbed on the surface of the carbon dots by electrostatic force of attraction, and under UV light irradiation, the silver ions are reduced to silver nanoparticles, which are gradually deposited on the surface of the carbon dots [31,32]. Fig. 1 illustrates the schematic diagram of the preparation process for F, N-CD@Ag.

The TEM images of the F, N-CD@Ag are shown in Fig. 2, which reveals well-dispersed, unobtrusively agglomerated particles of uniform size in F, N-CD@Ag-0.05 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 (Fig. 2a and d). The average size of Ag nanoparticles measured 4.54 nm and 5.12 nm (Fig. 2c and f), respectively. As the concentration of Ag increases, the Ag nanoparticles that are anchored by CD begin to aggregate, resulting in an average size of 8.67 nm for the F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 Ag nanoparticles (Fig. 2i). Studies indicate that the catalytic efficiency of the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) is highly contingent upon particle dimensions, with a pronounced escalation in activity as particle size diminishes. This reduction in size leads to an abbreviated diffusion path for reactants and the electrolyte solution. Moreover, an abundance of accessible and interactive active sites work in concert to bolster the overall electrocatalytic capabilities [33,34]. The HRTEM shows that most of the particles are amorphous carbon with no obvious lattice, and the surface shows rough amorphous areas with fuzzy lattice stripes, indicating the formation of abundant defects. The lattice stripes of the F, N-CD@Ag catalyst were clear and regular with a stripe spacing of 0.23 nm for the (111) crystalline surface of Ag (Fig. 2b, d, and e). It is found that the silver crystal plane (100) has a higher * OH adsorption energy than the crystal plane (111), resulting in excessive * OH coverage on the silver surface. On the one hand, it blocks the formation of * OOH, increasing 2 electron channels; On the other hand, it is not easy to desorb, which affects the efficiency of oxygen reduction catalytic reactions; The silver crystal plane (111) has relatively weak * OH adsorption and exhibits excellent catalytic performance for oxygen reduction [35]. Therefore, the crystal plane (111) of F, N-CD@Ag facilitates the improvement of ORR performance. Usually, heteroatoms with lone pair electrons (such as nitrogen atoms, etc.) are incorporated into the carbon framework [23,24], or the manipulation of surface defects on the carbon matrix [10], which can enhance the anchoring effect of the carrier on metallic silver, thereby enhancing the oxygen catalytic performance and stability.

In addition, the C, N, and F elements distribute homogeneously in the F, N-CD@Ag, as well as extremely small amounts of Ag residuals in F, N-CD@Ag-0.05 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 ( Fig. 3). While Ag element aggregated clearly in F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 as Ag concentration increases, which is not conducive to improving the catalytic performance of oxygen reduction. The aggregation of metal particles reduces the effective catalytic area and is not favorable to the diffusion of reactants on the catalyst surface. Therefore, the selection of appropriate silver content is crucial for the preparation of catalysts with good performance.

The XRD patterns ( Fig. 4) show that the characteristic peak at around 26° corresponds to graphitic carbon (002). Compared with CD, the (002) peak of F, N-CD, and F, N-CD@Ag shifted to a low angle, which indicated that the interlayer spacing was expanded by heteroatoms doping, facilitating the increase of active sites [12,36]. The appearance of new diffraction peaks at 38.1° and 44.3°correspond to the characteristic peaks of metallic silver, which are calibrated crystal plane (111) and (200), respectively. Literature reports the crystal plane (111) of Ag has relatively weak *OH adsorption, facilitating the generation of intermediate products *OOH and exhibiting excellent catalytic performance for ORR.

Raman spectroscopy is used to characterize the degree of graphitization and defects in catalyst materials, where the intensity ratio of the D-band to the G-band (ID/IG) is used to measure the degree of defects (Fig. 4). As observed in Fig. 4b, the strong D peaks in F, N-CD and F, N-CD@Ag are ascribing to abundant defects, which are generated by the introduction of a large number of functional groups and heteroatoms N on the surface of CD. Furthermore, the values of ID/IG decrease as the concentration of Ag increases, which could be attributed to the interaction between Ag and CD [37].

To further characterize the electronic state and local structure of the absorbing atoms, XPS is collected to study the electronic interactions between metallic silver and the carrier CD ( Fig. 5). The Ag 3d XPS shows the electron interaction between the carrier and metal Ag element as well as the charge transfer. Specifically, the Ag0 and Ag δ+ at binding energy (BE) of 367.22 eV and 367.73 eV, respectively, whereas the F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 catalyst shows upshifted component 367.35 eV and 367.82 eV, respectively. The reason could be that the interaction between F, N-CD, and Ag nanoparticles [24,38], which is ascribed to the size, the defect severity, and resulting in differences in catalytic performance. The high-resolution N 1 s peaks of F, N-CD@Ag catalysts were deconvoluted into pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N species and Oxidized-N shown in Fig. 5b. The F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 demonstrates enhanced ORR performance owing to the heightened concentration of pyridinic-N active species(Table1). Subsequently, the edge positioned pyridinic-N in dangling bonds of CD allow the formation of strong Ag-N bonds via enhancing the metal particles attachment to the carbon frameworks [39].

Additionally, it is suggested that the C-F semi-ionic form is extra effective to the ORR via polarizing the adjoining carbons to a better scope synergistically with N atoms [39,40], resulting in increased oxygen adsorption triggered oxygen breakage and established synergistic impact with N. Moreover, the semi-ionic state of fluorine (F) triggers a non-uniform aggregation of spin and charge, which effectively strengthens the interactions at the boundary between the neighboring carbon (C) and nitrogen (N) atoms27. In this scenario, the semi-ionic and ionic forms of fluorine assume the role of electron sinks. Consequently, this facilitates the redistribution of charge across the fluorine and the carbon framework. As a result, the increased C-F semi-ionic form (Table1) leads to an improved conductivity, which in turn, favors an increased degree of intercalation and ORR performance.
pyridinic-N, pyrrolic-N, graphitic-N and oxidized-N in F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2, respectively.

Fig. 6a-b shows the CV and LSV curves of F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1, and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 catalysts in O2/N2-saturated 0.1 M KOH. It is indicated that the ORR performance of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1, and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 are more outstanding than commercial 20 % Pt/C catalysts. The results compared to F, N-CD are self-evident [30]. The oxygen reduction peaks of F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 are located at 0.84 V, 0.85 V and 0.83 V, respectively. The onset potential and half-wave potential are 1.056 V and 0.90 V, which shift positively by 58 mV and 70 mV than that of F, N-CD@Ag-0.05. Moreover, it is proved that the catalytic performance of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 is far superior to that of similar systems [41,42]. With the increase of Ag concentration, the half-wave potential of F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 shifted negatively by 50 mV and the limited current density decreased by 0.42 mA cm−2, which could be the result of Ag nanoparticle aggregation. Thus, the carbon-based silver catalysts prepared from hybridized carbon-dot-anchored silver nanostructure have superior electrochemical properties, and F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 exhibits the best performance. The excellent electrochemical performance of F, N-CD@Ag could be attributed to the redistribution of charges between adjacent carbon atoms and changes in the local electronic structure, which could be ascribing to the introduction of hetero N and F atoms with lone pair electrons [23,24]. This can effectively adjust the center position of the silver d-band and strengthen the Ag-O interaction, thus facilitating the ORR performance and stability [25,26].

To further reveal the electrocatalytic activity of ORR on the catalyst, the reaction kinetics of ORR on F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1, and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 were investigated by rotating disk electrode (RDE). Fig. 6d shows the ORR measurements of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 at different rotation speeds (from 400 rpm to 2025 rpm) in 0.1 M saturated KOH solution. According to the slope of the K-L diagram, the electron transfer number (n) of the catalyst in the range of 0.675 V ∼ 0.725 V was obtained (Fig. 6f), exhibiting a nearly 4e- transferring process. The F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 catalyst exhibits the best catalytic performance among them, which is due to the weaker adsorption energy of *OH on the silver (111) surface compared to the (110) crystal surface, which is conducive to the formation of intermediate product *OOH and the desorption of the final product.

In order to evaluate the dynamic properties of ORR, the Tafel slopes of different samples were obtained by LSV curves, as shown in Fig. 6g[43]. The Tafel slope of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 is 69 mV dec−1 at 0.1 M KOH, which is lower than that of commercial Pt/C (79 mV dec−1), indicating that the F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 exhibits faster kinetic process and better catalytic activity. The ORR kinetics of F, N-CD @ Ag-0.1 electrocatalyst was further evaluated by fitting the EIS results with a simplified equivalent circuit (Fig. 6h). As we know, the semicircle in the low-frequency range is assigned to the charge transfer resistance (Rct), where the smaller the semicircle, the faster the charge transfer. We can easily get F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 to F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 to F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, and the diameter of semicircle is increasing, indicating that F, N-CD @ Ag-0.1 can accelerate the transfer kinetics of intermediate ions and electrons. F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 has been shown to exhibit improved ORR kinetics, which is consistent with the above LSV and Tafel results [44].

The potential-determining step of ORR on F, N-CD is the desorption of OH* η=0.52 V as shown in Fig. 6i. Meantime, the OOH* adsorption step (O2*→OOH*) also has a large potential barrier η=0.49 V; While the height of the O2*→OOH* step is decreased to −0.14 V for the configuration of F, N-CD-Ag due to the enhancement of the OOH* adsorption, which is caused by the introduction of Ag and the height of desorption of OH* is decreased to η=0.24 V. And OH* adsorption step (O*→OH*) is the potential-determining step of ORR η=0.39 V. The excellent ORR activity of F, N-CD could be the charge transfer during catalytic reactions, and the strong interaction between Ag and F, N-CD carrier. By doping CD with N and F atoms, the charge distribution of adjacent carbon atoms is redistributed, leading to changes in the local electronic structure. This can effectively adjust the center position of the silver d-band, strengthen the Ag-O interaction, not only affect the interaction between the carrier and silver, but also promote the adsorption of reactants and intermediates, increase the intrinsic activity of active sites, and achieve the goal of improving the electrocatalytic performance of ORR.

The stability of as-prepared catalysts is assessed by ADT. As shown in Fig. 7, there is a slight change in the peak potential of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 after 5000 cycles of CV, which to some extent reflects excellent stability. Furthermore, the current density of F, N-CD@Ag remains 97.5 % after 50,000 s, while for Pt/C, it reduces by 23.5 % after 50, 000 s. These results confirm the excellent ORR stability of F, N-CD@Ag in alkaline media, which undoubtedly cannot be separated from the strong anchoring effect of nitrogen-doped carbon dots on metallic silver.

In this paper, F, N-CD@Ag-0.05, F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 catalysts were prepared with F, N-CD as the carrier. Compared with the ORR catalyst attached to metal particles, the Ag nanoparticles prepared by this method have smaller sizes, more uniform distribution and excellent ORR catalytic performance. The results show that the onset potential and half-wave potential of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 are 1.056 V & 0.90 V, 58 mV & 40 mV, and 66 mV & 50 mV positive than those of F, N-CD@Ag-0.05 and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2. The current density of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 (6.5 mA cm−2) is 1.44 times and 1.07 times that of F, N-CD@Ag-0.05 (4.5 mA cm−2) and F, N-CD@Ag-0.2 (6.1 mA cm−2), respectively. Meantime, the ADT test indicates that F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 shows excellent stability than that of Pt/C. The outstanding ORR catalytic activity and stability of F, N-CD@Ag-0.1 could be attributed to the strong interaction between Ag and CD, ascribing to the strong anchoring effect of nitrogen-doped CD on Ag. The strong interaction promotes charge transfer during the catalytic reaction process, and this interaction can also maintain the stability of the catalyst center structure by suppressing the migration of metal atoms. This study provides good theoretical guidance for designing carbon nanomaterial-based catalytic systems.

Min Sun: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Siyu Chu: Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Haiyan Wang: Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zijiong Li: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Feng Miao: Software, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
